items.fullDescription: Marianne Moore 2027, hosted by the Faculty of English at the University of Cambridge, will follow the highly successful formats of previous Moore conferences (Penn State 2003, Houston 2015, Buffalo 2020, Stanford 2025) with a schedule of panels, round tables and Q&As for a single audience over two and a half days. Exactly 100 years after Moore herself visited Cambridge, the purpose of the conference is to bring together an international and various group of readers with a strong predilection for thinking and writing about poetry, and put them to work newly and again on this singular, difficult, and discriminating poet. The schedule will take shape around readings between Marianne Moore and other writers.

items.fullDescription: Abstracts are invited for a traditional panel session to be held at the annual meeting of the South Atlantic Modern Language Association, scheduled for 5-7 November 2026 at the Wyndham Atlanta Buckhead Hotel and Conference Center, Atlanta, GA, USA. This session intends to explore the theme of “hospitality” in the works of Joseph Conrad in order to highlight how Conrad’s relationships both reflected and influenced his literary output throughout his career. Some relationships were more enduring than others, but all had an impact, often a profound impact, on his life and writing.

items.title: “The Grandeur That Was Rome”: The 18th International F. Scott Fitzgerald Society Conference, June 21-28, 2027
items.excerpt: Rome and Capri, 21–28 June, 2027 F. Scott Fitzgerald visited Italy on two distinct occasions: first in the spring of 1921 during his and Zelda’s long-deferred honeymoon trip, and again from late 1924 to early 1925 during his unsuccessful attempt to economize. While the Fitzgeralds made brief stops in Venice, San Remo, Portofino, Savona, Pisa, Fiesole, Flo...
items.fullDescription: Rome and Capri, 21–28 June, 2027 F. Scott Fitzgerald visited Italy on two distinct occasions: first in the spring of 1921 during his and Zelda’s long-deferred honeymoon trip, and again from late 1924 to early 1925 during his unsuccessful attempt to economize. While the Fitzgeralds made brief stops in Venice, San Remo, Portofino, Savona, Pisa, Fiesole, Florence, and Naples, it was in the two host sites of this conference—Rome and Capri—that he spent the most time. It was here that he found inspiration for some of his most celebrated works, as well as several others that remain underexplored.

items.title: Global Koreas Early Career Mentoring Project
items.excerpt: The Global Asias Initiative (GAI) and Institute for Korean Studies (IKS) at the Pennsylvania State University (PSU), in partnership with UC-Irvine’s Center for Critical Korean Studies (CCKS), are conducting a mentoring project designed to foster a network of scholars working on Korean and Korean diasporic topics under the conceptual rubric of Global Asias...
items.fullDescription: The Global Asias Initiative (GAI) and Institute for Korean Studies (IKS) at the Pennsylvania State University (PSU), in partnership with UC-Irvine’s Center for Critical Korean Studies (CCKS), are conducting a mentoring project designed to foster a network of scholars working on Korean and Korean diasporic topics under the conceptual rubric of Global Asias. The goal of this project is to build a community of early-career scholars who are working at the intersections of Korean studies and Global Asias approaches; and to encourage intellectual exchange, collaboration, and the development of new critical approaches to Korean studies, Korean American studies, and Korean diaspora studies.

We are seeking papers that address Caro Claire Burke’s breakout debut novel Yesteryear and its implications on literature and current American culture. How does Burke address established literary conventions? How might one conceive of the evocativ...
items.fullDescription: We invite you to submit an abstract and bio for the panel “ Yesteryear: Cultural and Literary Possibilities”. We are seeking papers that address Caro Claire Burke’s breakout debut novel Yesteryear and its implications on literature and current American culture. How does Burke address established literary conventions? How might one conceive of the evocative narrative style of this novel? How does Burke conceptualize the conscription of motherhood, suppressed queerness, and conservative Christianity? How might the work fit into literary genealogies of sanity, purity, racial prejudice, and womanhood? Proposals should include a title, abstract, your name, and affiliation.

items.title: International Interdisciplinary Conference H.G. Wells and Modernity: Texts, Contexts, (Re)visions
items.location: Olsztyn, Poland · University of Warmia and Mazury in Olsztyn
items.excerpt: The Department of English-Language Literatures and Cultures and the Institute of Literary Studies at UWM are pleased to invite you to an international interdisciplinary conference on H.G. Wells and Modernity: Texts, Contexts, (Re)visions 22nd-24th Oct. 2026 University of Warmia and Mazury in Olsztyn, Poland Celebrating H.G. Wells's 160th/80th anniversarie...
items.fullDescription: The Department of English-Language Literatures and Cultures and the Institute of Literary Studies at UWM are pleased to invite you to an international interdisciplinary conference on H.G. Wells and Modernity: Texts, Contexts, (Re)visions 22nd-24th Oct. 2026 University of Warmia and Mazury in Olsztyn, Poland Celebrating H.G. Wells's 160th/80th anniversaries of birth and death (1866-1946), this conference invites scholars from across literary studies, linguistics, film studies, political theory, sociology, history, philosophy, and other disciplines to examine modernity's (con)texts through Wells, his contemporaries, and his followers. We aim to consider how their visions reshape understandings of the evolution and devolution of the human, humanity, and society. In many of his literary and non-literary texts, Wells diagnosed the shortcomings of class structures and anticipated modernity's ills, imaginatively prefiguring many current ways of conceptualising the human in relation to the nonhuman. He started as one of the most prolific and versatile short story writers of the late nineteenth century, and he ended as one of the most celebrated sociological and political writers of the first half of the twentieth century. His views, shaped by a complex and troubled faith in science and reason, helped crystallize the formulation of pivotal documents such as the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. He died disillusioned that the new world which he had envisioned had not arrived, or worse, frustrated to see his darkest predictions materialise. Undermined and still overlooked as a literary figure by the modernists, who could not see eye to eye with him despite initial friendships, who is H.G. Wells for posterity, that is, our times? In asking this question, we recognize that Wells's legacy cannot be confined to Anglophone literary history alone, and it still speaks urgently to contemporary crises of technology, social fragmentation and political imagination. We especially welcome approaches that could help us position Wells and other modernity/modernist writers within transnational literary networks and explore their influence on and dialogue with world literatures. These topics and others are most welcome, among the proposed conference focus points related to H.G. Wells: ? Author's sources: from antiquity to Jonathan Swift, Charles Dickens, Edgar Allan Poe, and others. ? Modernity and the new ways of writing: short story, scientific romance, science fiction. ? Modern aesthetics and the fictional world: literary and linguistic patterns. Representations of the human and humanity: ecocritical, cognitive, postcolonial, and other postmodern and post-postmodern re-readings. ? Wells and international fantastic and science fiction: comparative perspectives across French (Jules Verne), German, and other contexts. ? Wells's legacy in Polish literature: Stanis?aw Lem and others. ? Dialogues and contentions with Joseph Conrad, E.M. Forster, Virginia Woolf, and Henry James. ? Literary and linguistic depictions of science, catastrophe, and war. ? Shifting cultural concepts: evolution and devolution, tensions of power, ambiguities of colonial appropriation. ? Intermedia reinterpretations: film, graphic novels, games, and other media. ? Wells in translation. ? Political thought and the concept of world state. ? Models of femininity and social change. ? Wells and his engagement with history and the future. ? Utopias and dystopias of Wells and other writers. Proposals for a twenty-minute paper (about 200 words) and a bio note (100 words) should be sent to hgwellsconference@uwm.edu.pl by 10th September 2026. Notification of acceptance by 14th September 2026. Conference fee (non-refundable) payable by 20th September 2026. The account number will be provided upon acceptance. Conference languages: English, French, and Polish Standard fee: 180 Euro Fee for PhD students, students and accompanying persons: 130 Euro Conference Chair: dr hab. Halszka Lele? Secretary: dr Micha? Urbanowicz Advisory Board: prof. dr hab. Ewa Kujawska-Lis prof. dr hab. Iwona Ndiaye dr hab. Aneta Jachimowicz, prof. UWM dr hab. S?awomir Studniarz, prof. UWM Organizing Committee: dr Anna Kwiatkowska dr Dorota G?adkowska dr Eliza G?adkowska dr Dominika Kotu?a mgr Trevor Hill Student Research and Initiative Group "Anglo-Cooltura Juniors" We look forward to seeing you in Olsztyn TRANSPORT: Olsztyn is easily accessible from four main airports: ? Closest Option: from the airport Olsztyn-Mazury (SZY), take the direct airport train (PolRegio) to Olsztyn G?ówny (1 hour). Taxi transport is also possible, see below. ? From Warsaw Modlin (WMI): book a direct Kondratowicz shuttle for the fastest 2- hour trip: https://kondratowicztransport.pl/en/timetable/ ? From Warsaw Chopin (WAW): take a direct Kondratowicz shuttle from the terminal to Olsztyn (approx. 3h), or an SKM train to Warsaw Central station and then an Intercity train to Olsztyn's main station, called Olsztyn G?ówny (approx. 2h20). There are trains every two hours. ? https://kondratowicztransport.pl/en/timetable/ ? From Gda?sk (GDN): book a Wagner Transport shuttle (approx. 2.5h) or take a train to Olsztyn G?ówny via Gda?sk G?ówny (approx. 3h). https://wagnertransport.pl/ Train connections: https://rozklad-pkp.pl/en Long-distance bus services: FlixBus https://global.flixbus.com/ , PKS Olsztyn https://pks.olsztyn.pl/ , Sindbad https://www.sindbad.pl/en/home . Public transport in Olsztyn: https://www.olsztyn.com.pl/komunikacja.html Elf Taxi tel. 19622; Blue Taxi Olsztyn tel. 690 619 995; Bolt (book via app); Uber (also via app). ACCOMMODATION Hotel Park - located in a quiet area near the forest and university campus. Perfect for those seeking a peaceful stay, just a few minutes' walk from the Conference venue. https://hotelepark.pl/en/ Hotel Wile?ski - a boutique hotel situated in the historic centre, close to the Old Town and main attractions. https://hotelwilenski.com/en/ Best Western Plus - a comfortable and centrally located 3-star hotel in the heart of Olsztyn, a 3-minute walk from the historic Old Town. items.title: BAAS Postgraduate Symposium at the V&A: Transatlantic Connections and the ‘Special Relationship[s]’
items.location: V&A East Storehouse · British Association for American Studies
items.excerpt: Transatlantic Connections and the 'Special Relationship[s]': BAAS Postgraduate Symposium, 2026 V&A East Storehouse, London 9th November In 1946, Winston Churchill declared the need for a "special relationship between the British Commonwealth and Empire and the United States" in order to maintain world diplomacy, citing our shared language and cultural val...
items.fullDescription: Transatlantic Connections and the 'Special Relationship[s]': BAAS Postgraduate Symposium, 2026 V&A East Storehouse, London 9th November In 1946, Winston Churchill declared the need for a "special relationship between the British Commonwealth and Empire and the United States" in order to maintain world diplomacy, citing our shared language and cultural values as connecting the two countries. Although this 'special relationship' has continually been called into question by scholars, there is undoubtedly a channel of cultural and political exchange between the US and the UK, as well as with other countries, which has become even more urgent for discussion in recent years. The 2026 BAAS Postgraduate Symposium invites proposals from postgraduate researchers at all levels and across all disciplines on the topic of Transatlantic Connections and the 'Special Relationship[s]'. By using the term 'Special Relationship[s]', we intend to explore the relationship between not only the US and the UK, but also between the US and other countries. We invite panels and individual papers on this theme and encourage you to interpret it as broadly as you wish, including any aspect of the US's transnational cultural, political and literary connections, influences or contrasts. We welcome contributions from all realms of American Studies, including, but not limited to: literature and film studies, postcolonial and decolonial analysis, science and visual culture, and urban and environmental history. As this year's symposium will be held at V&A East Storehouse, the site of the David Bowie Archive, we are particularly keen on hearing from scholars exploring the etymologically and culturally rich prefix of the term 'Transatlantic' itself. This one-day symposium will involve not only academic panel discussions, but other activities such as a career development session, an introduction to the archives at the Victoria and Albert Museum, and a keynote address by Professor Mark Ford. The intention is to foster a strong postgraduate community in American Studies and to learn from both our peers and from leaders in our field. The symposium will be catered, hosted primarily in-person at V&A East Storehouse, but with a digital attendance option for those only able to join online. There will be time in the day to explore V&A East Storehouse and the David Bowie Centre. V&A East Storehouse is a working museum store with a rich archive for students to use in their research. The building is designed to allow visitors to get closer to objects held in the V&A collections and to the work of the museum itself. We welcome the submission of individual papers as well as proposals for complete panels of three researchers. Applications from groups of three or four researchers for roundtable discussions are also welcome. For panel and roundtable proposals, please also include a statement of up to 200 words on the aims of the collective submission, alongside the individual papers. Papers should be around 15-20 minutes long , and roundtables will consist of five-minute introductions by each researcher followed by 45 minutes of discussion. Interdisciplinary paper, panel and roundtable proposals are encouraged. Suggested paper and roundtable topics include, but are not limited to: - Case studies or broader comparisons between literary texts from the US and other nations - Case studies or broader comparisons between films from the US and other nations - Comparative political or historical analyses concerning the US and other nations - Comparative economic, scientific or environmental analyses concerning the US and other nations. Although the symposium will centre on 'Transatlantic Connections', we welcome proposals making any transnational comparisons rooted in American Studies. We especially encourage researchers from across Europe and beyond to submit and attend, as part of ongoing efforts to expand the EAAS community of postgraduates. We want to make this symposium as accessible as possible; we will be working to ensure a relaxed academic environment and we encourage anyone attending to email us with any access needs. Bursaries will be available from BAAS on a limited basis for students requiring financial support for attendance. The deadline for submissions is? 15th September 2026 .?Please send proposals including a title, an abstract of no more than? 250 words ?and?a short biography of no more than 200 words ?to Claire Watt at?claire.watt@baas.ac.uk. If you have any questions about the symposium, big or small, please get in touch and we will be happy to help. We look forward to hearing from you!

items.fullDescription: About the Book Indian Cultural Heritage: Perspectives is an edited volume that invites interdisciplinary scholarship on the diverse and dynamic dimensions of India's cultural heritage. The volume welcomes original contributions exploring tangible and intangible heritage, indigenous knowledge systems, languages, literature, folklore, performing arts, architecture, material culture, cultural memory, museums, heritage conservation, digital heritage, and contemporary issues of identity, sustainability, and cultural preservation. By bringing together diverse academic perspectives, the volume seeks to advance critical understanding of India's rich cultural traditions and their continuing significance in a rapidly changing world.

items.title: The Chorus: Blackness, Visitation, and the Poetics of Enclosure (ACLA '27)
items.location: Andrew Santana Kaplan, University of Denver / Joy Priest, University of Pittsburgh
items.excerpt: This virtual seminar takes its inspiration from Saidiya Hartman’s citation of the Greek etymology of the word chorus : “to dance within an enclosure.” For Hartman, the chorus also furnishes a paradigm for thinking—to play on Jean-Luc Nancy’s notion—Black (non)being singular plural. In Wayward Lives , Hartman suggests that any of the minor figures can stan...
items.fullDescription: This virtual seminar takes its inspiration from Saidiya Hartman’s citation of the Greek etymology of the word chorus : “to dance within an enclosure.” For Hartman, the chorus also furnishes a paradigm for thinking—to play on Jean-Luc Nancy’s notion—Black (non)being singular plural. In Wayward Lives , Hartman suggests that any of the minor figures can stand in for the others, not as a claim of homogeneity, but rather as a contention that their singularity can be read as paradigmatic of waywardness—in its queer heterogeneity—in general. The chorus of black femme and queer youth is thus at once a singular plurality and a plural singularity as they make beautifully riotous lives within the enclosure of social death.

items.title: NeMLA 2027 In and Out of Media – Intermediality in the Humanities of Today
items.excerpt: Historically, researching and teaching the different art forms took place in a well-sealed vacuum: for example, using literary theory to decipher one of the great texts, visual and compositional analysis to examine a sublime painting, or montage splicing to perceive the nuances of film. However, it is becoming more difficult to recognize the borders that...
items.fullDescription: Historically, researching and teaching the different art forms took place in a well-sealed vacuum: for example, using literary theory to decipher one of the great texts, visual and compositional analysis to examine a sublime painting, or montage splicing to perceive the nuances of film. However, it is becoming more difficult to recognize the borders that separate cinema, literature, and the visual arts. Factors including the rise of globalized societies and the emergence of new technology have forced us to reconsider medium specificity and our relationship with creative expressions and works of art.

items.title: Inclusive Teaching and Digital Accessibility: Working Toward a Similar Goal Through Multiple Means
items.sourceName: UPenn CFP: Humanities Computing and the Internet
items.excerpt: Inclusive teaching and digital accessibility are two important concepts that align with Universal Design for Learning (UDL) principles; they move away from "retrofitting" (i.e., fixing a problem after a student struggles) toward a "proactive” design (e.g., building a system that works for everyone from the start). Inclusive teaching is a pedagogical frame...
items.fullDescription: Inclusive teaching and digital accessibility are two important concepts that align with Universal Design for Learning (UDL) principles; they move away from "retrofitting" (i.e., fixing a problem after a student struggles) toward a "proactive” design (e.g., building a system that works for everyone from the start). Inclusive teaching is a pedagogical framework that focuses on designing a learning environment where every student (regardless of race, gender, socioeconomic status, or learning style) feels valued and has an equal opportunity to succeed. Whereas digital accessibility is a technical standard that ensures that digital tools, documents, and platforms are usable by everyone, regardless of ability.

items.fullDescription: As Artificial Intelligence becomes an integral part of higher education, AI literacy is increasingly recognized as a foundational competency for students, educators, researchers, and institutions. items.title: Slip into "The Backrooms"

items.excerpt: This panel session seeks to explore the cultural fascination surrounding "The Backrooms," an internet 'creepypasta' about an abandoned office building with endless corridors that became a popular YouTube series and most recently, a blockbuster horror film. What are the Backrooms? Do they represent an artificial or alien intelligence attempting to simulate...
items.fullDescription: This panel session seeks to explore the cultural fascination surrounding "The Backrooms," an internet 'creepypasta' about an abandoned office building with endless corridors that became a popular YouTube series and most recently, a blockbuster horror film. What are the Backrooms? Do they represent an artificial or alien intelligence attempting to simulate reality? Are they a glitch in the imperfect fabric of an unstable universe? Are they a metaphor for the subconscious, techno-paranoia, wasteland necrocapitalism, corporate control, or the internet itself? Or is this all just a retelling of the Greek myth of the Labyrinth for a Gen Z audience?

items.title: Digital Dalits and Global Solidarities: Collaborative Action in the 21st Century (Roundtable)
items.sourceName: UPenn CFP: Humanities Computing and the Internet
items.excerpt: This roundtable discusses the convergence of literary expression and digital activism as means of empowering a community. The shift of passive representation to active agency in the 21st century can be common in the digital space where the local knowledge is stored and disseminated. We discuss how marginalized groups, namely Dalit writers and activists, h...
items.fullDescription: This roundtable discusses the convergence of literary expression and digital activism as means of empowering a community. The shift of passive representation to active agency in the 21st century can be common in the digital space where the local knowledge is stored and disseminated. We discuss how marginalized groups, namely Dalit writers and activists, have used digital humanities to evade authoritarian gatekeepers and develop transnational solidarities. This session welcomes people to talk about how online spaces can be seen as a form of regenerative space where the testimonios of Dalit can be heard, transforming the world into a supportive space of collective action and literary insurgency.

items.title: The Rewritten Self: Counter-Narratives and Resistant Aesthetics
items.excerpt: On January 19, 2022, Sheila Heti published the first of ten weekly installments of “A Diary in Alphabetical Order” in The New York Times. Each installment included an author’s note explaining that Heti had “loaded all 500,000 words of [her] journals into Excel to order the sentences alphabetically,” hoping to “see the self in a new way.” Over time, the pr...
items.fullDescription: On January 19, 2022, Sheila Heti published the first of ten weekly installments of “A Diary in Alphabetical Order” in The New York Times. Each installment included an author’s note explaining that Heti had “loaded all 500,000 words of [her] journals into Excel to order the sentences alphabetically,” hoping to “see the self in a new way.” Over time, the project “grew into something more novelistic,” and the note concludes that “the self’s report on itself is surely a great fiction.” Heti is hardly the first author to challenge or destabilize autobiographical conventions.

items.title: New Directions in Italian Literature and Motherhood Studies
items.location: Laura Lazzari (Georgetown University) and Giulia Po DeLisle (UMass Lowell)
items.excerpt: Drawing inspiration from Adrienne Rich's seminal book Of Woman Born (1976), in 2006, Andrea O'Reilly coined the term "Motherhood Studies" and established a new interdisciplinary academic field aimed at studying "motherhood" as an institution and "mothering" as an experience. O'Reilly emphasized the importance of adopting a feminist framework designed expr...
items.fullDescription: Drawing inspiration from Adrienne Rich's seminal book Of Woman Born (1976), in 2006, Andrea O'Reilly coined the term "Motherhood Studies" and established a new interdisciplinary academic field aimed at studying "motherhood" as an institution and "mothering" as an experience. O'Reilly emphasized the importance of adopting a feminist framework designed expressly for mothers: a "matricentric feminism" necessary for an intersectional exploration of motherhood. Within "Motherhood Studies," being or not being a mother matters, and the experience of motherhood is treated as a category alongside gender, class, and race.

Building upon Hortense Spillers’ important essay Mama’s Baby, Papa’s Maybe (1987), we seek papers from humanities scholars in the French and Francophone worlds that reframe the body not as a passive victim but as a l...
items.fullDescription: This panel explores archives beyond the traditional frame and instead as embodied spaces of cultural memory and histories inscribed on skin. Building upon Hortense Spillers’ important essay Mama’s Baby, Papa’s Maybe (1987), we seek papers from humanities scholars in the French and Francophone worlds that reframe the body not as a passive victim but as a living text, a site of memory, and an alternative space that rescripts official records. By retracing histories through the reading of the body, we can activate affect, unleash unexpected memories, and create generative spaces for art that centres on cultural knowledge to empower communities.

items.title: When Lives Do Things: Lives as Archives
items.excerpt: As Sidonie Smith and Julia Watson argue, “autobiographical storytelling is a performative act through which narrators construct rather than simply describe selves.” In recent times, “life writing” has become a familiar term in literary and cultural studies, encompassing a range of written texts, including autobiographies, biographies, letters, memoirs, sl...
items.fullDescription: As Sidonie Smith and Julia Watson argue, “autobiographical storytelling is a performative act through which narrators construct rather than simply describe selves.” In recent times, “life writing” has become a familiar term in literary and cultural studies, encompassing a range of written texts, including autobiographies, biographies, letters, memoirs, slave narratives, prison narratives, captivity narratives, travel narratives, confessionals, conversion narratives and even in recent times, personal diaries. However, rather than remain transparent records of lived experience, life writings actively shape identities, produce historical knowledge, preserve cultural memory, and intervene in social and political worlds.

items.title: The Politics and Ethics of Literature in French Thought
items.excerpt: The guiding thread of this panel develops around a curious remark by Jacques Rancière that would seem to amend a classically Aristotelian thesis: “man is a political animal because he is a literary animal,” the fundamental condition of which is the “excess of words” constitutive of the human animal’s politicalness. Literature (or poetry) has long been pla...
items.fullDescription: The guiding thread of this panel develops around a curious remark by Jacques Rancière that would seem to amend a classically Aristotelian thesis: “man is a political animal because he is a literary animal,” the fundamental condition of which is the “excess of words” constitutive of the human animal’s politicalness. Literature (or poetry) has long been placed in close proximity to politics, as we find in Plato’s Republic , where philosophy and literature—and, more generally, philosophy and politics—turn out to be perennial adversaries.

items.fullDescription: While tragedy and horror are often treated as distinct genres, both traditions ask audiences to look steadily at what ordinarily repels them. From unburied corpses and undead monsters to violated kinship and failed agency, these spectacles produce fear, pity, disgust, and sorrow alongside fascination. This session explores what might be understood as a tragedy–horror continuum, examining the aesthetic, affective, and ritual overlaps between tragic and horrific representation across historical periods and media.

items.title: History and the Black Renaissance

items.fullDescription: As its title indicates, this seminar will take a broad geographic and temporal view of the Harlem or New Negro Renaissance to invite considerations of the historicity and historiography of Black writing at the turn of the twentieth century. Seminar participants will be invited to consider how history shaped and was shaped by Black art, literature, and thought in the period stretching from approximately the end of Reconstruction through the Civil Rights era. How did Black writers, artists, and thinkers use historical concepts, forms, narratives, figures, events, philosophies, discourses, and other materials to craft original works of art and literature?

items.title: NeMLA Convention Panel: Planetary Crises in Unequal Worlds: Environmental Justice Across Cultures, Media, and Languages
items.excerpt: Panel Proposal for the NeMLA Convention, Newport, RI, March 6–9, 2027 Planetary crises—climate change, toxic pollution, biodiversity loss, and energy transition—are profoundly uneven in their distribution. Their material consequences are shaped by infrastructures of class, race, colonial histories, state power, and global capitalism. Increasingly, literar...
items.fullDescription: Panel Proposal for the NeMLA Convention, Newport, RI, March 6–9, 2027 Planetary crises—climate change, toxic pollution, biodiversity loss, and energy transition—are profoundly uneven in their distribution. Their material consequences are shaped by infrastructures of class, race, colonial histories, state power, and global capitalism. Increasingly, literary, artistic, and cultural texts across the world foreground these asymmetries, framing ecological crisis not only as environmental catastrophe but as a question of justice: Who breathes polluted air? Who profits from green transformation? Who consumes melting ice? Who bears the social and environmental costs of planetary change?

items.title: Reproductive (Un)silencing: Empowering Communities Through Creative Writing
items.excerpt: In the definition given by Podnieks, matrifocal literatures are written and narrated by mothers in the first-person or limited third-person voice, rendering maternal identity and experience from subjective perspectives. Such narratives (re)value the significance and meaning of maternal figures in cultural, social, and national arenas, and often contest or...
items.fullDescription: In the definition given by Podnieks, matrifocal literatures are written and narrated by mothers in the first-person or limited third-person voice, rendering maternal identity and experience from subjective perspectives. Such narratives (re)value the significance and meaning of maternal figures in cultural, social, and national arenas, and often contest or negotiate traditional ideologies of the “good” mother as self-sacrificing, nurturing, and sexless with her antithesis, the “bad” mother. In the last few years, we have seen the rise of matrifocal writings published to break taboos about previously silenced reproductive experiences, create a sense of community, support, and care, and, overall, empower people.

items.fullDescription: This panel invites proposals that examine acts of remembrance and care of an individual’s or a collective’s past that lead to empowerment and change. Papers may address historical or contemporary works in art, architecture, film, recordings, and literature, and may engage theoretical, critical, pedagogical, or archival practices. Drawing from Valérie Perrin’s novels Forgotten on Sundays (2015) and Fresh Water for Flowers (2019), soon to be on the screen by Jean-Pierre Jeunet, memory work enables individuals to feel visible, situate themselves in a collective, and solve piece puzzles through inquiry. Such work is often relegated to women’s labor, caregivers, and hired help who go beyond roles and job duties to make personal connections.

items.fullDescription: Philip Kan Gotanda’s play Yankee Dawg You Die centers a conflict between two Asian American actors. The aging Vincent Chang built his career playing bumbling Japanese soldiers and one-dimensional Asian stereotypes in classic Hollywood. He’s confronted by the up-and-coming Bradley Yamashita, who insists that Asian (American) characters should be played with dignity and accuracy. The heart of the play is an ambivalence about Asian (American) representation and whether viewers should sympathize with Chang for taking the only acting jobs available or with Yamashita in his condemnation of the racist roles—or with both.

items.fullDescription: In bell hooks’ Belonging: A Culture of Place (2009), she posits that “many folks feel no sense of place” (1). She continues by explaining her personal experience with this spatiality, noting that, “Like many of my contemporaries I have yearned to find my place in this world, to have a sense of homecoming, a sense of being wedded to a place” (2). To that end, a commonality shared between all humans is a searching for a place in which we belong, and this is particularly evident in literature that is produced during times of major societal events.

items.title: Rewriting the Canon: A Look at What We Learn from Contemporary Updates of the Classics
items.excerpt: Since 1991 the Pulitzer Prize has been awarded to four novels that reimagined literary works that have long been a part of the canon. items.fullDescription: Since 1991 the Pulitzer Prize has been awarded to four novels that reimagined literary works that have long been a part of the canon. They are: A Thousand Acres by Jane Smiley (a contemporary King Lear ); March by Geraldine Brooks ( Little Women from the perspective of the absent father); Demon Copperhead by Barbara Kingsolver (David Copperfield set in Appalachia); and James by Percival Everett (Adventures of Huckleberry Finn from the perspective of Jim). This panel looks to open a discussion about how these and other contemporary novels both explore and challenge the themes of the source material. What do they say about society, race, gender, feminism, and marginalization?

items.title: Edith Wharton & the Power of Community
items.excerpt: In both her life and her literary work, American writer Edith Wharton was a keen observer and recorder of the interrelationship(s) between power and community at the end of the 19th century and throughout the early decades of the 20th. Born into the world of wealth and privilege of “Old New York” society, Wharton grew weary of its longstanding communal no...
items.fullDescription: In both her life and her literary work, American writer Edith Wharton was a keen observer and recorder of the interrelationship(s) between power and community at the end of the 19th century and throughout the early decades of the 20th. Born into the world of wealth and privilege of “Old New York” society, Wharton grew weary of its longstanding communal norms and constraints. At the same time, however, she was also openly critical of the ostentation and conspicuous consumption on display during the annual Newport, RI “summer season.”

items.title: Autonomous Black Worldmaking as Community Empowerment
items.excerpt: In her resolute commitment to telling stories about Black life without the white gaze, Toni Morrison articulated a model of community empowerment grounded in local knowledge, intra-communal collaboration, and the self-reflexive narration of Black worlds. While Morrison remains one of the most visible public advocates of this aesthetic and ethical orientat...
items.fullDescription: In her resolute commitment to telling stories about Black life without the white gaze, Toni Morrison articulated a model of community empowerment grounded in local knowledge, intra-communal collaboration, and the self-reflexive narration of Black worlds. While Morrison remains one of the most visible public advocates of this aesthetic and ethical orientation, practices of Black worldmaking across African and diasporic literatures have long exceeded mere response to anti-Blackness. Even under conditions structured by racial violence, coloniality, displacement, and social rupture, Black communities have continually generated modes of life, relation, memory, and imagination that assert the possibility of worlds otherwise.

items.title: Communal Narrative Forms in South Asian Fiction and Indigenous Traditions (Panel-NeMLA 58th Annual Session March 6–9, 2027)
items.excerpt: Beecroft argues in The Cambridge Companion to the Novel that, if we understand the novel as storytelling in any narrative form indigenous to a particular culture or people, then we must acknowledge the existence of multiple novels, each with distinct temporal and spatial origins. He maintains that storytelling traditions are embedded in every society, and...
items.fullDescription: Beecroft argues in The Cambridge Companion to the Novel that, if we understand the novel as storytelling in any narrative form indigenous to a particular culture or people, then we must acknowledge the existence of multiple novels, each with distinct temporal and spatial origins. He maintains that storytelling traditions are embedded in every society, and that many of these traditions have complemented, intersected with, or diverged from Western narrative forms (46–48). However, the narrative form canonized as the “novel” in the Western literary tradition has a specific historical and aesthetic origin in ancient Greece.

items.title: Oral Community Knowledge as Antidote for Empire in Anglophone Literature
items.location: Newport, Rhode Island · Northeastern Modern Language Association (NeMLA)
items.excerpt: CFP for NeMLA 2027 Conference Virtual Only Panel Oral Community Knowledge as Antidote for Empire in Anglophone Literature If Empire thrives and even conquers through written text: codification of laws and bureaucracy, cartography of territory, travelogues of the exotic Other, it equally seeks to suppress oral indigenous knowledge of the vanquished. Yet or...
items.fullDescription: CFP for NeMLA 2027 Conference Virtual Only Panel Oral Community Knowledge as Antidote for Empire in Anglophone Literature If Empire thrives and even conquers through written text: codification of laws and bureaucracy, cartography of territory, travelogues of the exotic Other, it equally seeks to suppress oral indigenous knowledge of the vanquished. Yet orality can also be the site of resistance and community identity for just such a reason. This panel seeks proposals of works that investigate how orality of form and/or content in Anglophone literature disrupts Empire and empowers community. The irony of pursuing orality in literature, which is historically written, is not lost on us, but also provides an opportunity for creative, resilient exploration. Some possible ways of approaching this topic may include: Accent and dialect Pidgins, creoles, and contact languages Translations and translators Language and identity Oral folk wisdom and stories Glossaries and indices Textual hybridity or multimodality Literacy This is a Call for Papers for the NeMLA 2027 Conference. Submissions will only be accepted through the NeMLA portal, with a deadline of September 30, 2026 . This panel is Virtual Only , regardless of whether the participant is physically present at the conference. Paper proposals can be submitted here: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22648. Only submissions submitted through the NeMLA portal will be considered. For questions, please feel free to email Dr. Dorisa Costello at dcostello@jessup.edu.

items.title: Reading the Earth: Geology, Memory, and Relational Poetics
items.excerpt: Across contemporary literary and cultural studies, increasing attention has been devoted to the ways in which geological matter shapes cultural imagination, environmental memory, and literary form. Rocks, minerals, fossils, sediments, ruins, and other more-than-living material formations are no longer understood merely as settings or symbols, but as archi...
items.fullDescription: Across contemporary literary and cultural studies, increasing attention has been devoted to the ways in which geological matter shapes cultural imagination, environmental memory, and literary form. Rocks, minerals, fossils, sediments, ruins, and other more-than-living material formations are no longer understood merely as settings or symbols, but as archives of deep time, environmental change, historical violence, and planetary entanglement. This panel invites papers exploring how literature engages with the Earth as a site of memory, relation, and meaning-making. We are particularly interested in approaches that examine geological formations, landscapes, material traces, planetary archives, and sedimented histories as participants in literary and cultural processes rather than passive backdrops to human experience. Possible topics include geologies of memory; deep time and literary temporality; environmental trauma and material archives; fossils, ruins, and traces; sacred landscapes and earth-based imaginaries; extraction, waste, and environmental justice; posthumanist and new materialist approaches to literature; relational poetics; and the intersections of geology, ecology, and cultural memory. We welcome contributions addressing literary and cultural texts from any historical period, linguistic tradition, or geographical region, as well as comparative and transnational perspectives. The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. Please clearly indicate in your submission whether you wish to participate in person or remotely. All abstract submissions must be submitted through the NeMLA portal https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22630. Please note that the submission window for individual abstracts officially opens June 15 and closes September 30.

items.title: Africana Integrativa: Locating the “Black” in Bad Bunny
items.excerpt: This panel presents a review of the musical, artistic, literary, social, political, and cultural expressions of Bad Bunny, Benito Antonio Martinez Ocasio. The panel centers on aspects of African diasporic experiences, i.e., Afro-Latinidad. The panel considers Blackness as gleaned and embedded from Bad Bunny's musical repertoire and uses Aurora Santiago Or...
items.fullDescription: This panel presents a review of the musical, artistic, literary, social, political, and cultural expressions of Bad Bunny, Benito Antonio Martinez Ocasio. The panel centers on aspects of African diasporic experiences, i.e., Afro-Latinidad. The panel considers Blackness as gleaned and embedded from Bad Bunny's musical repertoire and uses Aurora Santiago Ortiz and Jorell Melendez-Badillo's framework of "historical memory" and "limits of nostalgia" as presented in their piece "(Nada es) 'Como antes.'" The panel seeks to utilize this framework and focus on Black presence in all its intersecting and non-intersecting forms involving race, class, gender, and sex. Specifically, papers are sought that examine strategies and uses of, for example, an "idyllic past" and/or "Great Mixed Family" motif and show ruptures in representations that subsume or otherize Blackness. Submissions can interpret outside of Bad Bunny and include other artists venturing into its use in other realms and/or aspects of appreciation or appropriation discourse, read broadly and sublimated in text. The panel discusses the uses of various musical, visual, aural, and literary works to deconstruct language use, song beats, films, concerts, and media, similar to Bad Bunny's use of House, Hip Hop, Trap, Drill, Reggaeton, Bachata, Afro Punk, etc. A key element is locating, gleaning, or demystifying aspects of Blackness, construed broadly, in Bad Bunny's context or in other works that offer perspectives on Black embeddedness and involve authenticity, appreciation, and the intersection of race, class, gender, or sex. Papers are sought that review the application, infusion, or parallelism of the Black past infusing Bomba, Plena, Son, Salsa, Trap, Drill, Reggaeton, and other genres, as merged running threads. This panel invites papers that explore the musical, artistic, literary, social, political, and cultural expressions of Bad Bunny (Benito Antonio Martinez Ocasio). We focus on African diasporic, or AfroLatinidad, experiences. The panel examines how Blackness construed in various forms appears in Bad Bunny's music and/or is subsumed within it. The panel delves into the intersections of race, class, gender, and sex, and the recognition of the African presence, identity, or representation.

items.title: Medievalisms in Times of (mis)information
items.location: International Society for the Study of Medievalisms
items.excerpt: In 1996, Carl Sagan warned us of a time when people would lose their ability to question, “when awesome technological powers are in the hands of a very few . . . when, clutching our crystals and nervously consulting our horoscopes, our critical faculties in decline, unable to distinguish between what feels good and what’s true, we slide, almost without no...
items.fullDescription: In 1996, Carl Sagan warned us of a time when people would lose their ability to question, “when awesome technological powers are in the hands of a very few . . . when, clutching our crystals and nervously consulting our horoscopes, our critical faculties in decline, unable to distinguish between what feels good and what’s true, we slide, almost without noticing, back into superstition and darkness.” Maybe Mr Sagan was somewhat pessimistic when writing The Demon-Haunted World. But then again, in times of conspiracy theories, Ancient Aliens, Antivaxxers, and Holocaust Deniers, the Age of Information appears just as capable of spreading dangerous frauds and charlatans as it is of disseminating reliable scholarship.

items.title: Large loose baggy monsters: Communities in the multiplot horror
items.excerpt: When Henry James described the Victorian multi-plot novel as “large loose baggy monsters,” he didn’t have twentieth and twenty-first century horror in mind, but the echoing effects of the Gothic on writers like Charles Dickens, Wilkie Collins, Mary Elizabeth Braddon, Charles Reade, and others. These writers drew on the multiplied plots and perspectives of...
items.fullDescription: When Henry James described the Victorian multi-plot novel as “large loose baggy monsters,” he didn’t have twentieth and twenty-first century horror in mind, but the echoing effects of the Gothic on writers like Charles Dickens, Wilkie Collins, Mary Elizabeth Braddon, Charles Reade, and others. These writers drew on the multiplied plots and perspectives of the Gothic to represent how social worlds were constructed and constrained.

items.title: Apocalypse or Utopia: Whose End of the World?
items.excerpt: Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. Primary Areas of interest: Global Anglophone / Cultural Studies and Media Studies Abstract: Since at least the middle of the 20th century, we have seen few, if any, utopian texts speculating about positive potential worlds. Instead, we have seen a deluge of dystopia...
items.fullDescription: Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. Primary Areas of interest: Global Anglophone / Cultural Studies and Media Studies Abstract: Since at least the middle of the 20th century, we have seen few, if any, utopian texts speculating about positive potential worlds. Instead, we have seen a deluge of dystopian, apocalyptic, and post-apocalyptic texts foretelling the end of the world. This begs the question, why have utopian texts become such a scarce literary genre? What is it about our time that makes speculative literature turn away from the utopic and instead toward its more nihilistic counterparts? Our societal fascination with apocalyptic literature has not gone unnoticed. Writing in 1979, H. Bruce Franklin identifies this cultural anxiety in his essay, "What Are We to Make of J. G. Ballard's Apocalypse," where he claims that the post-World War II decolonial liberation movements and the collapse of the European empires were the source of this apocalyptic obsession. On the other hand, the utopian genre is often acknowledged as beginning with Thomas More's Utopia (1516), written within the burgeoning rise of capitalism and the colonial expansion of early 16th century Europe. Hythloday, More's fictional narrator, finds Utopia within the New World, where the undiscovered territories and peoples outside of Europe became the catalysts that made the early utopian imagination possible. That the European utopian genre came at the expense of the existing worlds of indigenous peoples might also prompt us to think about how apocalypse factors into the utopian imagination. Since Franklin's essay, this fixation with the end of the world has only intensified, driven to new heights by the overwhelming evidence of climate change's devastation, global pandemics, and catastrophic geopolitical conflicts. Franklin concludes that Ballard's novels are unable to make a distinction between the end of capitalism and the end of the world itself. This prompts us ask: whose "world" is ending and how might it make way for a new one?

items.title: Literary and Cultural Communities Between Asia and the Hispanophone World
items.excerpt: From the early European missionaries who ventured into Asia all the way to the contemporary networks of connection that currently link Spain and Latin America countries like China, Japan and Korea, the cultural, literary and artistic links between Asia and the Hispanophone world run deep. Based on religious, linguistic, cultural, literary or artistic poin...
items.fullDescription: From the early European missionaries who ventured into Asia all the way to the contemporary networks of connection that currently link Spain and Latin America countries like China, Japan and Korea, the cultural, literary and artistic links between Asia and the Hispanophone world run deep. Based on religious, linguistic, cultural, literary or artistic points of contact, numerous groups and communities have maintained active and productive lines of connection that have transform their own localities through the implementation of imported ideas, products, concepts from the other side of the world. Considering the long-standing lines of dialogue between these two vast geographic spaces, we invite scholars reflecting on questions such as: How Hispanophone writers and artists have searched in Asian literary practices spaces for renewal and innovation? How communities from Asian countries have assimilated and reinterpreted Latin American cultural products? How do the cultural products and creative practices of the two sides of the Pacific Ocean mirror/distort/reinvent each other? Papers dealing with literature, theatre, cinema, and other cultural productions are welcome. This is board-sponsored panel accepts presentations in English and Spanish are welcome.

items.title: Bodies, Borders, and Belonging: Comparative Perspectives on South Korean and Italian Women Writers
items.excerpt: This panel explores the intersections between contemporary South Korean and Italian women's writing through the lenses of memory, gender, trauma, migration, and post-national identity. While these literary traditions are often studied separately within distinct geopolitical and linguistic frameworks, the panel proposes a comparative and transnational appr...
items.fullDescription: This panel explores the intersections between contemporary South Korean and Italian women's writing through the lenses of memory, gender, trauma, migration, and post-national identity. While these literary traditions are often studied separately within distinct geopolitical and linguistic frameworks, the panel proposes a comparative and transnational approach that foregrounds shared concerns surrounding patriarchal structures, historical violence, intergenerational memory, and the negotiation of belonging. Bringing together scholars working on Italian and Korean women writers, the panel examines how literature becomes a space for articulating marginalized experiences and challenging dominant national narratives. The selected texts engage with themes such as family silences, colonial and postwar legacies, diaspora, racialization, feminist activism, labor, and the body as an archive of memory. Through memoir, autofiction, and experimental narrative forms, these writers construct alternative genealogies and forms of counter-memory that complicate homogeneous understandings of identity and nationhood. The panel seeks to foster an interdisciplinary conversation between Asian Studies, Italian Studies, Comparative Literature, Gender Studies, and Migration Studies. By placing South Korean and Italian women writers in dialogue, the session highlights the productive possibilities of comparative feminist methodologies across different cultural contexts while emphasizing shared strategies of resistance, care, and narrative reconstruction.

items.title: Resisting from the Edge: Marginal Characters and Collective Empowerment
items.location: Salve Regina University, Newport, RI · NeMLA
items.excerpt: This panel invites papers that examine the agency of marginalized characters in literary, visual, audiovisual, and artistic productions from the French?speaking world across any historical period. Artistic works offer numerous examples of figures who maintain an ambivalent relationship to their communities. Because of their social or ethnic origins, their...
items.fullDescription: This panel invites papers that examine the agency of marginalized characters in literary, visual, audiovisual, and artistic productions from the French?speaking world across any historical period. Artistic works offer numerous examples of figures who maintain an ambivalent relationship to their communities. Because of their social or ethnic origins, their divergent perspectives, or their transgression of established norms, certain characters find themselves pushed to the margins of social, familial, or professional groups. While such exclusion may appear to weaken those who experience it, this panel asks whether marginalization might also function as a source of strength. Can marginality become a generative position? Do marginalized individuals succeed in forming new communities that enable them to confront stigma and ultimately overcome it? This discussion seeks to shift our understanding of the place occupied by marginalized individuals and groups in fictional narratives. By considering the networks of solidarity that emerge among marginalized subjects, the panel explores what lies beneath their presumed inferiority, inviting critical attention to what is hidden, erased, or rendered invisible within dominant narratives. More importantly, we will examine how marginalized communities may become empowering spaces of resistance, mutual support, and hope. Drawing on approaches from cultural studies, postcolonial theory, sociolinguistics, and intersectionality, this session seeks to understand how literary, visual, audiovisual, and artistic narratives stage the tensions between marginality and belonging, strength and vulnerability. Proposals engaging with environmental humanities, posthumanism, Indigenous studies, as well as diaspora and migration studies are strongly encouraged. We also welcome submissions drawing on gender and queer studies, memory and trauma studies, digital humanities, or other interdisciplinary frameworks within the humanities. We welcome proposals of 250-300 words, in French or English, submitted by September 30.

items.title: Subtle Performances of the Body: Female Agency and Autonomy in Literature
items.excerpt: How do female characters employ their bodies to navigate, process, and challenge the cultural and socioeconomic structures of their times? Under the antagonistic system where the pursuit of "good life" is no longer plausible, how do women across different cultures, historical periods, and geopolitical contexts respond by utilizing their bodily autonomy an...
items.fullDescription: How do female characters employ their bodies to navigate, process, and challenge the cultural and socioeconomic structures of their times? Under the antagonistic system where the pursuit of "good life" is no longer plausible, how do women across different cultures, historical periods, and geopolitical contexts respond by utilizing their bodily autonomy and agency? This panel invites papers that critically investigate female bodily representation in the context of empowerment and change. In particular, this panel is interested in literary analyses that focus on the subtle yet tangible (dis)orientation of female bodies in and out of public spaces. Gestures, clothing, behavior, decorum, and adherence to social respectability are tactical ways in which female characters maneuver the contours of their precarious belonging to the surrounding environment. We welcome papers that extend the existing scholarship on female agency and bodily autonomy as a crucial means of cultural transformation. This (in-person) panel invites papers that critically investigate female bodily representation in the context of empowerment and change. In particular, this panel is interested in literary analyses that focus on the subtle yet tangible (dis)orientation of female bodies in and out of public spaces. https://cfplist.com/nemla/ Home/S/22387

items.title: "Why Am I Reading This?" Finding Literary Relevance
items.excerpt: If you've taught older literature recently, you've probably heard students ask the question "Why am I reading this?" They've got a point. Why should we read Shakespeare's plays, or Paradise Lost , or (god-forbid) "The Rape of the Lock"? We might be able to coax students into reading a Jane Austen novel or even Huckleberry Finn because they are still part...
items.fullDescription: If you've taught older literature recently, you've probably heard students ask the question "Why am I reading this?" They've got a point. Why should we read Shakespeare's plays, or Paradise Lost , or (god-forbid) "The Rape of the Lock"? We might be able to coax students into reading a Jane Austen novel or even Huckleberry Finn because they are still part of our cultural imagination, but it's less easy to coax students into reading Beowulf or Tom Jones -texts that are less familiar in form and content. As we jettison the canon and the timeless truths of literature, what do we expect reading older texts will give our students that they wouldn't otherwise understand? How do we explain our reasoning to students about why they should read texts from earlier literary periods? How do we present these earlier periods (or periodization) and their culture and ideas as relevant to our undergraduate students in our English, writing, or humanities programs? Or is relevance the problem? How might we redefine older literature in our classes, or what we (can) do with these texts? I'm particularly interested in position papers that take into consideration practical pedagogical and theoretical concerns: arguments about curricula, course development or assignments that do different things with this literature. Papers are welcome on any kind of literature/texts or periods prior to 1800.

items.title: Collective Selves: Rethinking Autobiography Beyond the Individual.
items.location: Salve Regina University, New Port Rhode Island · NeMLA
items.excerpt: Collective Selves: Rethinking Autobiography Beyond the Individual. Philippe Lejeune's influential formulation of the autobiographical pact has long shaped autobiography studies. The pact presumes an aligned author, narrator, and protagonist, and simultaneously privileges the singular, first-person life narrative as the normative autobiographical form. How...
items.fullDescription: Collective Selves: Rethinking Autobiography Beyond the Individual. Philippe Lejeune's influential formulation of the autobiographical pact has long shaped autobiography studies. The pact presumes an aligned author, narrator, and protagonist, and simultaneously privileges the singular, first-person life narrative as the normative autobiographical form. However, several personal narratives resist these assumptions. Edited autobiographies, oral histories, collaborative memoirs, and interview-based narratives often construct an autobiographical "I"/protagonist that is multiple rather than singular and that emerges across the voices of individuals whose experiences collectively narrate a shared history. Likewise, some collective life narratives depart from conventional autobiography structures, appearing as fragmented accounts, interwoven testimonies, or dispersed chapters within larger texts. This panel invites papers that examine forms of self-representation that foreground the collective. Particularly of interest are papers that interrogate the presumed unity of author, narrator, and protagonist; reconsider the autobiographical pact in light of collaborative and collective forms of narration; and explore how communities write themselves into history through shared acts of remembrance, testimony, and self-representation. Topics to consider include, but are not limited to: Collaborative and edited autobiographies Oral histories and interview-based life narratives Community and collective testimony Indigenous, Black, and diasporic collective life writing Gender, Sexuality and self testaments Archives, memory, and community self-representation Theoretical reconsiderations of the autobiographical pact and authorial voice Experimental and hybrid forms of autobiography Collective authorship, voice, and narrative authority Life writing, activism, and community formation. By centering collective modes of self-account, this panel seeks to expand the boundaries of autobiography studies and establish interventions that consider how communities, rather than individuals alone, become autobiographical subjects.

items.title: Empowering Communities: Multi-Modalities of Voice, Word, and Non-verbal Expressions
items.location: Newport, Rhode Island · Northeast Modern Language Association
items.excerpt: Language is key to communication, whether visual, auditory, linguistic, spatial, or gestural. In literature, modalities might include devices such as phraseology, tone, sensory elements, symbolism, satire, irony, characterizations, or figurative language. Modalities in literature can be narrative, descriptive, persuasive, expository, causal, effectual, de...
items.fullDescription: Language is key to communication, whether visual, auditory, linguistic, spatial, or gestural. In literature, modalities might include devices such as phraseology, tone, sensory elements, symbolism, satire, irony, characterizations, or figurative language. Modalities in literature can be narrative, descriptive, persuasive, expository, causal, effectual, definitive, comparative, and argumentative. Through close and careful examination of "voice," "word," or "non-verbal expressions" of speaker or writer, someone might discover surprising probabilities, poignant certainties, unexpected opportunities, awakened obligations, absurd necessities, or unusual permissions that shift from "a state of passivity to being active agents of change." This roundtable session will explore, define, determine, and discuss how key modalities in various literatures have the ability to excite, intrigue, and empower communities--large and small, academic and personal, collective and individual-to values of positivity and collectiveness.

items.title: Liminal Dialogues: AI, Voice, and Impossible Conversations in Literary Creation
items.location: Newport, RI Convention Site: Salve Regina University · NEMLA
items.excerpt: This creative session invites scholars, writers, and hybrid practitioner-theorists to explore liminal dialogue as a form of literary creation in which unlikely, displaced, or structurally incompatible voices are brought into conversation. Drawing on the centrality of dialogue in literary and dramatic traditions, this panel reimagines conversational form a...
items.fullDescription: This creative session invites scholars, writers, and hybrid practitioner-theorists to explore liminal dialogue as a form of literary creation in which unlikely, displaced, or structurally incompatible voices are brought into conversation. Drawing on the centrality of dialogue in literary and dramatic traditions, this panel reimagines conversational form as a site of constraint-based experimentation, where meaning emerges through the collision of discourses that would not ordinarily coexist. Rather than focusing on artificial intelligence as a technological subject, this session treats AI-assisted generation as a formal device for producing estranged adjacency between voices, genres, and epistemologies. What happens when characters drawn from radically different narrative worlds, historical moments, or discursive systems are placed in structured conversation? How does meaning shift when dialogue is detached from stable context, shared reality, or conventional narrative logic? We welcome creative-critical submissions that use AI-assisted or procedural methods to generate liminal dialogues between incompatible speakers or speech forms. Contributions might include conversations between fictional, historical, or conceptual figures who would not normally meet; dialogues staged between competing textual systems or genres; or experimental works in which voice, tone, and argumentative logic destabilize one another through forced proximity. Submissions may also explore the failure of communication itself as a productive literary space. The session is especially interested in work that frames dialogue as a site of interpretive tension rather than narrative coherence, and in which meaning emerges through friction, misalignment, and discursive instability. AI or generative systems may be used as compositional tools, but the emphasis should remain on literary form, voice construction, and interpretive effect rather than technology itself. We encourage submissions across poetry, prose, hybrid writing, and performative or experimental formats suitable for conference presentation.

items.title: Paris Noir: Changes in the Margin
items.excerpt: This panel seeks papers on artistic movements, individuals or events that demonstrate how living as an outsider or in exile can facilitate cross-cultural learning and creating. The call is based on ideas of reterritorializing lost, outside, and minoritarian cultures such as the Négritude movement-which was recently celebrated at the center of the Paris-No...
items.fullDescription: This panel seeks papers on artistic movements, individuals or events that demonstrate how living as an outsider or in exile can facilitate cross-cultural learning and creating. The call is based on ideas of reterritorializing lost, outside, and minoritarian cultures such as the Négritude movement-which was recently celebrated at the center of the Paris-Noir exhibit at the Pompidou- and what Gertrude Stein calls the Lost Generation to find empowerment in exile. We seek to share techniques of research and teaching about the power of the powerless by studying overlooked artistic communities, individuals and cultures. For example we welcome submissions about how artists of the Paris Noir and/or Lost Generation relate to issues of culture, creating and community in our time. Many are unaware that James Baldwin, Miles Davis, and Jean-Michel Basquiat worked alongside Ernest Hemingway, Gertrude Stein and Henry Miller. These expatriates and African American artists electively inhabited the creative "Undercommons" of Paris Noir to remain generative despite fascism, racism, and war. These artists who were foreigners and outsiders are now classic and read inside the classroom by a majority in culture. Submit papers that analyze minorities, foreigners, strangers, exiles and outsiders in literature, film, or culture as a model for reflecting upon ways of reterritorializing the classroom and community. Below are some questions to orient your submission, although you are not limited to working on these: How does the marginalized turn the margin to a source of empowerment? What type of artist or work demonstrates change on the margins? What does it mean to inhabit to the role of foreigner, outsider, or minority? How does the existence of the stranger, immigrant, minority, or neighbor facilitate cross-cultural dialogue and generativity? How do we inhabit and engage vulnerability, process, child-like discovery, repetition and outside as a way of innovating that resists an authoritarian or artificial rule? Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. All abstract submissions must be submitted through the NeMLA portal. https://cfplist.com/nemla/ Home/S/22549 Chair: Dr. Jessica Datema, jdatema@bergen.edu, https://bergen.edu/academics/academic-divisions-departments/english-department/our-composition-literature-faculty/jessica-datema/ https://www.nemla.org/convention.html

Since then, however, an abundance of scholarship has yielded everything from formal...
items.fullDescription: In the four hundred years since its invention in Renaissance Florence, opera has become synonymous with the grandiose, the excessive, and the melodramatic, yet it has only gained a foothold in the academy as an object of serious academic study within the past fifty years. Since then, however, an abundance of scholarship has yielded everything from formal musicological readings of operatic works to theoretical inquiries inspired by psychoanalysis into voice and performance. And topics like the relationship between opera and sovereignty in seventeenth century Italy and the appropriation of Wagner by the Third Reich underscore how opera has never been far from the political sphere in the Western world. But as Slavoj Zizek confirms, "the more opera is dead, the more it flourishes." Opera's story may be one of perpetual decadence, but contemporary creators deserving of academic attention push against the narrative and economic forces that have challenged the validity of opera as an artform in the 21st century. This panel seeks to complement contemporary scholarship on opera across the humanities by soliciting papers that speak to opera's unique, overdetermined, and intrinsically interdisciplinary blend of words, music, performance, and history, as well as papers that examine operatic genealogies extant in non-operatic texts. Potential panelists are also invited to think across languages, borders, and performance traditions and consider the important social and political questions of canonicity, representation, and ownership as they pertain to opera and the operatic.

items.fullDescription: “I must be mad!” cries Elizabeth Costello at the close of J.M. Coetzee’s The Lives of Animals (1999). An author and animal advocate, Costello cannot reconcile how the atrocities of factory farming that keep her awake at night do not likewise haunt her peers. When picking up the character again over two decades later in The Pole and Other Stories (2023), Coetzee presents Costello in the grips of early dementia, which has brought her into even greater communion with the suffering of animals. As she discloses to her son, “I can’t put the image out of my mind, John.

items.title: Gendered Mobilities: Migration, Community and Belonging in World Literature
items.location: Newport, RI Convention Site: Salve Regina University · NeMLA's 58th Annual Convention
items.excerpt: Abstract The twenty-first century is continuously defined by geopolitical shifts, armed conflict, environmental precarity, and evolving border regimes, within which migration operates as a critical locus of global transformation. This physical transgression of geopolitical boundaries inevitably instigates a profound restructuring of individual and collect...
items.fullDescription: Abstract The twenty-first century is continuously defined by geopolitical shifts, armed conflict, environmental precarity, and evolving border regimes, within which migration operates as a critical locus of global transformation. This physical transgression of geopolitical boundaries inevitably instigates a profound restructuring of individual and collective subjectivities. Literature functions as a dynamic repository for these complex trajectories, articulating the psychological, political, and cultural exigencies of displacement and resettlement. Crucially, migration is a highly gendered phenomenon; an individual's positionality is negotiated and reconfigured at every stage of the diasporic process-from the structural catalysts for departure to the physical perils of transit and the socio-political parameters of host nations. Global literary traditions rigorously interrogate these intersections, offering critical frameworks to analyze the nuances of home, alienation, and hybridity. This session aims to examine how global narratives represent the friction between shifting geopolitical borders and the ontological reconstruction of the gendered self. Consequently, it intends to investigate how authors across diverse historical epochs, geographic regions, and genres textualize the quest for belonging. By transcending static regional paradigms, this session highlights the multi-faceted modalities through which literature maps human mobility. Description This session holds immense academic significance by applying an intersectional framework to global displacement. It bridges the analytical gap between individual trauma and macro-level geopolitical structures through the concept of community. Ultimately, it redefines world literature by treating textuality as a dynamic repository for marginalized subjectivities.

items.title: Reading and Representing Culture Through Foodways (Panel)
items.excerpt: Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. This panel invites contributions that examine how food and foodways shape and reflect cultural knowledge across time and place, with particular attention to the relationships among foodways, memory, migration, identity, cultural heritage, and community formation. Pr...
items.fullDescription: Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. This panel invites contributions that examine how food and foodways shape and reflect cultural knowledge across time and place, with particular attention to the relationships among foodways, memory, migration, identity, cultural heritage, and community formation. Proposals are welcome from a range of disciplinary and professional perspectives that explore food and cultural identity in literature, cinema, and other forms of media; oral history projects; mapping initiatives; community archives and digital collections; public-facing scholarship; and pedagogical approaches to Food Studies. The panel also invites discussion of emerging technologies and methodologies that offer new ways of researching, representing, and engaging with foodways and cultural heritage, while expanding opportunities for the communication and exchange of cultural knowledge. Whether through literary and cinematic representation, historical research, oral histories, or other forms of cultural production and inquiry, food-centred scholarship offers valuable insights into the ways individuals and communities understand and communicate culture across generations and geographies. In keeping with the conference theme, Empowering Communities, this session considers how foodways can serve as sites of cultural expression, knowledge-sharing, and collective memory, while encouraging reflection on questions of belonging, exchange, continuity, and cultural change.

items.fullDescription: In the Joy Luck Club , Amy Tan redefines the domestic space and the feminine laborassociated with it as a dynamic space where Chinese-American women reshape their identities, preserve their cultures, and share their stories of traumas. bell hooks’ assertion that “homeplace" was the one site where one could freely confront the issue of humanization” provides a productive framework for understanding how kitchens and acts of cooking become spaces of healing, survival, and female agency in women’s writing. By doing these, they try to assert a form of power, and agency that subverts the traditional notion of the domestic space(as an inferior space that is away from history), and the feminine labor associated with it.

What we don’t think about, however, is the immense literary scene of writers from the Central Valley and/or writing about the Central Valley. From Manuel Muñoz, to Gary Soto, to Juan Felip...
items.fullDescription: When we think of the Central Valley, that strange opaque space between Los Angeles and San Francisco, we often think of heat, smog, and sometimes fruits and vegetables. What we don’t think about, however, is the immense literary scene of writers from the Central Valley and/or writing about the Central Valley. From Manuel Muñoz, to Gary Soto, to Juan Felipe Herrera, to Lee Herrick, to Marisol Baca, to David Dominguez, to many others, the Central Valley literary community is capacious and deserves attention. As such, this panel session invites papers that discuss the literary community of the Central Valley.

Though sensationalization can be found in works of fiction, the threads of one’s reality—intersect...
items.fullDescription: In this modern moment, there have been a myriad of proposals exploring the contours of “speculative fiction”—be that social, environmental, &c. This session seeks to deconstruct the ways in which the speculative shapes the contours of the “real” or realized. Though sensationalization can be found in works of fiction, the threads of one’s reality—intersectional as it may be—provides a rich narrative that encourages further exploration and the fears provided in what we would safely relegate to fiction. However, there remains a “seeping out” of those fears that can eerily be represented in the real world.
